nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zones of Lake Malawi, associated with Bagrus catfish hosts; inhabits caves, crevices, and rocky outcrops along the lake margins

decorPreferences: large rocky structures with caves and crevices
dense rock stacking to create territories and hiding spots
minimal or no live plants (plants uprooted)
open swimming areas between rock piles

diet.notes: Specialized omnivore. In the wild primarily ectoparasites and mucus scraped from host catfish (Bagrus meridionalis and B. crassilabris), supplemented by small invertebrates, algae, and opportunistically the eggs and fry of its Bagrus host. In captivity accepts a wide variety of foods: high-quality cichlid pellets, spirulina flakes, Mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, and blanched vegetables. Avoid excessive animal protein and avoid beefheart/mammalian meat, which can cause Malawi bloat. Feed 2–3 small meals daily.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna of similar size (Melanochromis, Labeotropheus, Labidochromis)
large Aulonocara peacock cichlids in large display tanks
other Pseudotropheus species in large, heavily decorated tanks
avoidWith: other Pseudotropheus crabro males (severe territorial aggression)
community fish not adapted to hard alkaline water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Pseudotropheus crabro; shows adult male in typical bumblebee yellow-and-black barred coloration. Check current availability at accessedDate.
Wikimedia Commons image; verify current license attribution before use.

summary: A striking Lake Malawi mbuna with yellow-and-black barring, uniquely known for cleaning ectoparasites off Bagrus catfish and opportunistically raiding their nests. Highly aggressive; best kept in large, heavily-decorated African cichlid setups by experienced hobbyists.
careNotes:
The Bumblebee Cichlid (Pseudotropheus crabro) is one of the most behaviorally fascinating Lake Malawi mbuna, notable for a mutualistic-turning-parasitic relationship with the large catfish Bagrus meridionalis (kampango) and B. crassilabris. In its natural habitat it acts as a cleaner fish, removing ectoparasites and dead skin from these host catfish — a relationship beneficial to both parties — but it also opportunistically raids the Bagrus nest to consume eggs and newly hatched fry, turning the interaction predatory. This dual role has made it a subject of scientific interest and earns it the alternative name Chameleon Cichlid (also referencing its ability to rapidly shift between yellow-black barred and darker reversed coloration, used to approach host catfish undetected). In captivity the cleaning behavior is rarely expressed, but the color-change capacity is still observed under stress or during spawning.

A minimum 300 L tank with a 150 cm footprint is strongly recommended. Furnish with extensive rockwork arranged into caves and distinct territory zones; open sand between rock piles allows retreat. Live plants are impractical as they are routinely uprooted. Water chemistry must reflect the naturally hard, alkaline, and oxygen-rich conditions of Lake Malawi: pH 7.6–8.8, gH 10–20, temperature 23–27 °C. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are essential; Malawi Bloat is a common disease linked to poor water quality and excessive protein in the diet.

The species is highly aggressive and territorial, particularly between males. In a display tank, keep one male with several females and stock with other robust mbuna of comparable aggression and size. Males can be relentless in pursuit of subordinates; heavy rockwork providing sight breaks and multiple refuges is critical to survival of tankmates.

Taxonomic note: Pseudotropheus is now considered paraphyletic, and many former members of the genus have been moved to Maylandia (= Metriaclima), Tropheops, and Cynotilapia. P. crabro is retained in Pseudotropheus sensu stricto by most current authorities, but literature may use older synonymies.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is triggered by good water quality and conditioning on varied live and frozen foods. The male courts the female vigorously — often aggressively — and spawning occurs on flat rocks or in caves. The female collects the eggs in her mouth immediately after laying and incubates them for approximately 21–28 days at 26 °C. Broods typically number 20–50 fry. The female should be moved to a separate tank or provided with substantial cover during mouthbrooding to prevent male harassment and premature spitting of the brood. Fry are released fully formed and able to accept crushed flake and micro-pellets. Remove the female for recovery after fry are independent. Note: males in captivity will court and harass females near-constantly; a 1:3 or 1:4 male-to-female ratio significantly reduces stress on individual females.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudotropheus crabro
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (23–27 °C), pH (7.6–8.8), hardness (10–20 °H), native range (Lake Malawi rocky zones), diet (ectoparasites, mucus, Bagrus eggs in wild; varied in captivity), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day incubation), behavior notes including cleaner-fish/egg-thief duality, and tank recommendations. Key species page confirming retention in Pseudotropheus sensu stricto.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudotropheus crabro
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (to 15 cm TL), native range (Lake Malawi, Malawi and Mozambique), depth range (3–30 m reported), habitat (rocky littoral), and diet classification (invertivore/ectoparasite specialist). Confirmed brood parasite/cleaner fish status.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care
additional.notes: Supplemental trade source for general Lake Malawi mbuna husbandry guidance including tank sizing, water chemistry targets, diet cautions (Malawi Bloat, low protein), stocking strategies, and decor recommendations. P. crabro not covered individually; general mbuna guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pseudotropheus crabro species profile
additional.notes: Supplemental source. Covered the unique Bagrus host-association behavior (parasite-cleaning and egg-theft), the chameleon color-change mechanism, breeding details (maternal mouthbrooding, brood size), and careLevel assessment (intermediate to advanced for dedicated African cichlid keepers). Corroborated size, pH, and temperature data.

additional.name: Konings, Ad — Malawi Cichlids in their Natural Habitat (4th ed.), Cichlid Press
additional.notes: Expert reference for Lake Malawi ecology and mbuna natural history. Documented the Pseudotropheus crabro–Bagrus meridionalis cleaning and egg-predation interactions in detail, confirmed depth range and rocky biotope, and addressed the Pseudotropheus paraphyly and systematic revisions (Maylandia/Metriaclima split). Used to verify behavioral and ecological data.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 20–50 eggs/fry for 21–28 days. Separate brooding female to protect brood. Males highly aggressive toward females; keep 1 male to 3–4 females. Fry accept micro-crushed flake on release.
